objective.paragraph1: A chess game is played between two players: White and Black. The goal is to checkmate the opponent's King.

pieces.paragraph1: A standard game starts with 32 pieces in total (16 per side), with 6 piece types: King, Queen, Rook, Bishop, Knight, and Pawn.

moving-pieces.paragraph1: Players move alternately, one legal move at a time. Piece movement rules are:

king.paragraph2: Moves one square in any direction. The King may not move into check.

queen.paragraph2: Moves any number of squares horizontally, vertically, or diagonally, as long as no piece blocks the path.

bishop.paragraph2: Moves any number of squares diagonally and cannot jump over pieces.

rook.paragraph2: Moves any number of squares horizontally or vertically and cannot jump over pieces.

knight.paragraph2: Moves in an L-shape (two squares in one direction and one perpendicular). The Knight can jump over pieces.

pawn.paragraph2: Moves one square forward (or two squares from its starting rank if both squares are empty). Captures one square diagonally forward.

special-moves.paragraph2: Castling: King moves two squares toward a rook and the rook jumps over.
special-moves.paragraph3: En passant: special pawn capture right after an opponent pawn advances two squares.
special-moves.paragraph4: Promotion: when a pawn reaches the last rank, it promotes to Queen, Rook, Bishop, or Knight.

capturing.paragraph1: A piece captures by moving to a square occupied by an opponent piece. Captured pieces are removed from the board.
capturing.paragraph2: You cannot capture your own pieces. Except for the Knight, pieces cannot jump over other pieces.
capturing.paragraph3: The King is never captured. The game is won by checkmate, or ends by resignation/draw.

check.paragraph1: A King is in check when attacked by one or more opponent pieces. You must respond immediately.
check.paragraph2: To respond to check, a player may choose one of the following legal options:
check.paragraph3: Move the King to a safe square
check.paragraph5: Block the line of attack (if possible)
check.paragraph6: If none of these is possible, it is checkmate and the game is lost.

result.paragraph1: A game is won by checkmate, when the checked King has no legal escape.
result.paragraph2: If a player runs out of move time (in modes that use per-move time), that player loses.
result.paragraph3: If a player runs out of total game time, that player loses (unless the opponent has no possible mating sequence).
result.paragraph4: A game is drawn by stalemate, dead position (insufficient mating material), or draw agreement.
result.paragraph5: A game can also be drawn by the 50-move rule or threefold repetition according to the rules.
